read.image: won't you let an innocent woman be
read.take: She spends the whole thing roasting him and barely breaks a sweat doing it. The phone that wasn't broken, just uncharged. The outfit she prays is ironic. "Did you just say you're finished? Didn't know we started." Each line is a clean hit, and she keeps posing as the put-upon innocent woman who never signed up for any of this. Then the bridge turns the knife on her own hand. She likes her men incompetent. She likes her boys playing hard to get. And right after admitting it, she swears "they choose me, I'm not choosing them" — the denial too quick to believe. The portrait she's been drawing of him turns out to be a mirror, and "amen, hey, men" is half blessing, half surrender to the type she keeps going back to.
read.contradiction: She calls herself an innocent woman dragged into this and admits in the same breath that incompetent is exactly her type.

arc.label: The Roast
arc.scene: She's got the receipts and she's enjoying them. The dead phone, the outfit, the boy who announced he was finished before anything began. She names what's wrong with him and barely raises her voice, because there's a cuter word for it and she already knows what it is.

arc.label: The Innocent Act
arc.scene: Now she plays the martyr. Why does he always come running to her, this grown man with no concept of self-care and half a brain gone missing? She asks to be let be like a woman who had no part in it, and the halo she puts on is purely for show.

arc.label: The Tell
arc.scene: The bridge flips the whole song. She likes her boys hard to get, likes her men incompetent, and says so out loud. "I swear they choose me, I'm not choosing them" is the line that gives her away. The innocent woman wanted this all along, and she almost laughs admitting it.

lines.line: You said your phone was broken, just forgot to charge it
Did you just say you're finished? Didn't know we started
Half your brain just ain't there

And I like my men all incompetent
And I swear they choose me, I'm not choosing them

read.texture: A sunny, mid-tempo pop strut you can dance to, bright and major-key all the way through. The melody's so cheerful the insults slide down like a wink.
